Use Case Guide

Best Construction Software for Estimating (2026)

Better estimates mean fewer surprises. Here's the software that gets you there.

✓ Updated April 2026

Construction estimating is where jobs are won or lost before they start. Underestimate and you work for free; overestimate and you don't get the job. The right estimating software brings discipline to this process — consistent templates, accurate cost tracking, and the feedback loop that makes your next estimate better than the last.

Types of construction estimating tools

Not all estimating software does the same thing. Know the category before you buy:

  • PM platforms with estimating modules: Buildertrend, JobTread, Procore — full project management tools with integrated estimating. Best for contractors who want one platform from bid to closeout.
  • Dedicated estimating software: ProEst, STACK, PlanSwift — specialized tools focused purely on estimating and takeoff. More powerful for estimating specifically, but require integration with your PM tool.
  • Takeoff software: PlanSwift, STACK, Bluebeam Revu — tools for measuring quantities from plans digitally. Different from pricing software; you use them together.

Best estimating tools by contractor type

For residential contractors and remodelers: JobTread

Pricing: $99–$349/mo

JobTread's estimating workflow was clearly designed by people who understand how residential contractors actually bid jobs. Build an estimate with line items organized by cost category, apply markup per category (labor vs. materials vs. subs), and generate a professional client-facing proposal in one click. Client approves, estimate becomes the project budget automatically — no double entry.

This estimate-to-budget connection is the key differentiator. When the kitchen tile supplier delivers the wrong product and you need a change order, your budget reflects it instantly. When the job closes, you can see exactly which line items you got right and which you missed — making your next estimate more accurate.

Try JobTread — The cleanest estimate-to-budget workflow for residential contractors. Professional proposals, digital approval, automatic budget creation.

Visit JobTread →

For builders running multiple simultaneous projects: Buildertrend

Pricing: Complete $1,099/mo (full estimating suite)

Buildertrend's estimating capabilities on the Complete tier are the deepest available for residential construction. Sub bid management (soliciting bids from multiple subs per scope), bid leveling, and integration with the scheduling and financial management modules make it a complete pre-construction to closeout workflow. The price point ($1,099/mo for Complete) means this is for production builders and larger remodeling companies, not solo operators.

Try Buildertrend — Full bid management, sub solicitation, and estimating for builders running high-volume residential work.

Visit Buildertrend →

For commercial contractors: Procore Estimating

Pricing: Module add-on to Procore (custom pricing)

Procore's estimating module handles commercial bid management: soliciting sub bids, leveling competing bids, and managing the formal award process. It's designed for the multi-party commercial bidding environment where you're managing 15 sub bids per project across 8 divisions. Not relevant for residential contractors; the right tool for commercial GCs already on Procore.

For takeoff-heavy work: PlanSwift or STACK

PlanSwift pricing: ~$1,749/yr (~$146/mo)

STACK pricing: $2,999/yr (professional) or custom

If your estimating depends heavily on accurate material takeoffs from plan sets — framing, drywall, roofing, flooring, electrical — a dedicated takeoff tool pays for itself quickly. You load PDFs of the drawings and measure quantities directly on screen: linear feet, square footage, counts. The software calculates totals, which you then import into your estimating or PM software for pricing.

The time savings vs. manual plan measurements is significant. A job that takes 4 hours to estimate manually might take 90 minutes with digital takeoff. Over 50 estimates per year, that's real time recovered.

The estimate-to-actual feedback loop

The most valuable feature in estimating software isn't the estimate itself — it's the comparison between estimated and actual costs at job closeout. This feedback loop is what converts estimating software from a bid tool into a business intelligence tool.

Here's the pattern that works:

  1. Build estimate with job cost line items
  2. Client approves → estimate becomes project budget
  3. During the job, actual costs are entered (supplier invoices, labor hours, sub billings)
  4. At closeout, compare estimated vs. actual per line item
  5. Update your cost templates based on what you learned

Contractors who do this consistently — and JobTread and Buildertrend both support this workflow — bid more accurately over time. Contractors who don't are making the same estimating mistakes on every project.

Common estimating mistakes

No indirect cost allocation

Overhead costs (insurance, vehicles, tools, office) need to be recovered across jobs. If you're only pricing direct labor and materials, you're losing money on every job to overhead. Build your markup percentage to include these costs, or add overhead as a separate line item. Most construction estimating software supports both approaches.

Not updating cost data

Lumber prices in 2024 are different from lumber prices in 2021. If your material cost database hasn't been updated in two years, your estimates are wrong. Set a calendar reminder to review and update material costs quarterly.

Estimating without a template

Starting every estimate from scratch leads to inconsistency and forgotten line items. Build a template for each project type you do regularly (bathroom remodel, kitchen renovation, new home build). The template captures every scope item so nothing gets missed under bidding pressure.

Frequently asked questions

What's the difference between estimating software and bid management software?

Estimating software helps you determine your costs and build a price. Bid management software handles the process of soliciting prices from multiple subs, comparing their bids, and awarding work. Commercial GCs need both; residential contractors typically only need estimating + proposals.

Should I buy dedicated estimating software or use the module in my PM platform?

For most residential contractors, the estimating module in JobTread or Buildertrend is sufficient and eliminates the need to maintain a separate tool. For contractors doing high-volume complex bids, dedicated tools like ProEst offer more depth. Start with your PM platform's estimating module; only add dedicated software when you've hit its limits.
